sourceMovie.title: Hell or High Water
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Hell or High Water for the same reason it broke through in 2016: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a crime rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. David Mackenzie keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The western and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Appaloosa
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Hell or High Water clicked for you, Appaloosa (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in western and crime, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Thelma & Louise
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Hell or High Water clicked for you, Thelma & Louise (1991) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Hell or High Water?
faq.answer: Try Appaloosa, Thelma & Louise, Once Upon a Time in Mexico—each shares DNA with Hell or High Water while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Hell or High Water?
faq.answer: Hell or High Water (2016) is best described as Western, Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Hell or High Water worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this western lane.
